proposals.motivation: Beth Levine's lab established that beclin 1 (BECN1) is a haploinsufficient tumor suppressor, but the precise molecular adaptations that enable tumorigenesis in the context of partial autophagy impairment remain unclear. Standard differential expression analyses in such haploinsufficient contexts often fail to capture the subtle, systems-level rewiring of cellular pathways. Insights from plant stress biology, where network analysis of autophagy-deficient mutants revealed coherent compensatory programs, suggest a powerful, untapped approach to understand how mammalian tumors adapt to autophagy loss.
proposals.hypothesis: Tumors with beclin 1 haploinsufficiency activate specific, coordinated transcriptional modules—involving alternative degradation pathways, ER stress, and metabolic reprogramming—that compensate for reduced autophagy, and these modules share network properties with evolutionarily conserved stress-response programs.
proposals.method: We will apply Weighted Gene Co-expression Network Analysis (WGCNA) to existing microarray datasets from beclin 1 haploinsufficient tumor models and autophagy-competent controls. This will involve constructing signed co-expression networks, identifying modules of highly correlated genes, and correlating module eigengenes with the autophagy-deficient phenotype. Hub genes within significant modules will be identified via intramodular connectivity (kME), and their regulatory networks will be analyzed using pre-2026 tools like TRANSFAC or ENCODE data to pinpoint key transcription factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond gene lists to define the active transcriptional programs that sustain tumors with compromised autophagy, identifying novel hub regulators as potential therapeutic targets. It will also test the cross-kingdom conservation of stress-adaptation network logic, bridging plant cell biology and cancer biology.
proposals.novelty: The proposal innovatively adapts a network-analytic framework from plant stress biology to a cancer autophagy context, a direct cross-kingdom analogy not previously explored. It focuses on detecting systems-level compensation in a haploinsufficient setting, where effects are distributed and not easily captured by conventional differential expression.

proposals.title: A Functional Dependency Framework to Distinguish Driver from Passenger Alterations in Autophagy Genes Across Human Cancers
proposals.motivation: Pan-cancer genomic studies have cataloged frequent alterations in autophagy-related genes, but these events are often dismissed as passengers of widespread chromosomal instability. This conflation obscures the identification of true cancer-relevant autophagy drivers, hindering the development of targeted therapies. A systematic, functionally informed classification is urgently needed to separate biologically impactful alterations from genomic noise.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating genomic alteration patterns with functional dependency data from CRISPR screens and autophagy flux signatures will enable the robust classification of autophagy gene alterations as drivers or passengers, revealing context-specific cancer dependencies.
proposals.method: We will integrate TCGA pan-cancer genomic data (mutations, CNVs) with DepMap CRISPR/RNAi essentiality scores and transcriptomic autophagy flux signatures. A random forest classifier will be trained to rank alterations using features including mutation frequency, co-occurrence with CIN markers, essentiality scores, and survival associations. Top-ranked candidate driver genes (e.g., BECN1, ATG genes) will be validated in isogenic cell line panels using CRISPR-Cas9, measuring autophagy flux (LC3-II turnover), proliferation, and sensitivity to mTORC1 inhibitors.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first functionally annotated atlas of autophagy gene drivers in cancer, moving beyond descriptive catalogs. It will identify high-confidence, context-specific therapeutic targets within the autophagy pathway and provide a generalizable framework for classifying alterations in other complex biological processes.
proposals.novelty: This study moves beyond correlative genomic surveys by directly integrating functional dependency data to infer causality. The machine learning model is uniquely designed to deconvolute the signal of specific autophagy gene alterations from the background of chromosomal instability, a major confounder in prior analyses.

proposals.title: Single-Cell and Spatial Mapping of Autophagy Heterogeneity in Beclin 1 Haploinsufficient Tumors
proposals.motivation: BECN1 is a haploinsufficient tumor suppressor, but how its partial loss shapes intratumoral heterogeneity in autophagy activity remains unknown. This gap limits our understanding of how autophagy modulation contributes to tumor evolution and therapy resistance. The field currently lacks tools to resolve autophagy states at single-cell resolution within the tumor microenvironment.
proposals.hypothesis: BECN1 haploinsufficiency generates a functional mosaic of tumor cells with distinct autophagy competencies, and these autophagy-defined subpopulations occupy specific spatial niches that influence tumor progression.
proposals.method: We will perform scRNA-seq on BECN1+/- tumor models (e.g., MCF7, MDA-MB-231) and deconvolve autophagy states using curated gene modules (core ATG proteins, lysosomal genes, TFEB targets). We will apply RNA velocity (scVelo) to infer dynamic transitions between autophagy states. Spatial validation will be performed using 10x Genomics Visium and high-plex MERFISH for key autophagy markers to map state distributions relative to vasculature (CD31) and hypoxia (CA9).
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first high-resolution atlas of autophagy heterogeneity within a tumor, linking BECN1 status to cell state diversity. It will identify whether autophagy-low and -high subclones cooperate metabolically and establish a spatial framework for understanding autophagy in tumor microecology.
proposals.novelty: This adapts the deconvolution and spatial mapping paradigm from immunology to the autophagy field, a novel application. It moves beyond bulk measurements of autophagy flux to resolve single-cell states and their spatial organization, which has not been done.

proposals.title: Systematic Forward Genetic Mapping of Autophagy-Dependent Tumor Suppressor Networks in Drosophila and Zebrafish
proposals.motivation: Autophagy is a critical tumor-suppressive mechanism, but its regulatory network and interactions with oncogenic pathways remain incompletely defined. The field lacks a comprehensive, unbiased map of the genetic landscape governing how autophagy constrains tumorigenesis, analogous to the incomplete understanding of developmental signaling pathways prior to saturation mutagenesis screens. This gap limits our ability to predict which tumors are autophagy-dependent and to design targeted therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: Unbiased forward genetic screens in genetically tractable, non-mammalian tumor models will identify a conserved set of genes that functionally modulate autophagy-dependent tumor suppression, revealing novel nodes and pathways within this network.
proposals.method: We will perform EMS-based saturation mutagenesis in a Drosophila epithelial tumor model (e.g., RasV12, scribble-/- clones in the eye imaginal disc) and screen for modifiers of autophagy-dependent tumor phenotypes using established GFP-LC3 reporters and tissue overgrowth assays. In parallel, we will conduct CRISPR-Cas9-based loss-of-function screens in zebrafish models of BRAFV600E-driven melanoma or tp53-mutant tumors, using automated imaging to quantify tumor burden and autophagy flux (via transgenic mCherry-GFP-LC3). Conserved hits from both systems will be validated in mammalian cells (e.g., MEFs or cancer cell lines) using siRNA knockdown and assays for autophagy activity (LC3 lipidation, p62 degradation) and oncogenic transformation.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first comprehensive genetic interaction map for autophagy in tumor suppression across two evolutionarily distant model organisms, identifying novel conserved regulators. It will establish a foundational resource for the field, prioritizing genes for mechanistic study in mammalian systems and potentially revealing new therapeutic targets for cancers reliant on autophagy for survival.
proposals.novelty: While genetic screens for autophagy regulators exist, none have been performed as saturation-level, forward genetic screens in the specific context of established in vivo tumor models, an approach that mirrors the transformative developmental biology screens of the past. The parallel use of invertebrate and vertebrate genetic systems for this specific question is unprecedented.

proposals.title: Systematic Validation of GFP-LC3 Puncta as Specific Markers of Functional Autophagosomes
proposals.motivation: GFP-LC3 puncta formation is the most widely used assay for monitoring autophagy in cells. However, pre-2026 literature suggests LC3 can be recruited to protein aggregates (e.g., in aggresomes) and that certain conditions (e.g., proteasome inhibition, expression artifacts) can induce LC3-positive structures independent of canonical autophagy. This fundamental ambiguity undermines the interpretation of countless studies in autophagy, cancer, and neurodegeneration.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant fraction of GFP-LC3 puncta observed under standard experimental conditions, including stress and pharmacological modulation, are not functional autophagosomes but represent LC3-decorated protein aggregates or other non-degradative structures.
proposals.method: We will use live-cell imaging of GFP-LC3 in tandem with specific fluorescent probes: 1) mCherry-SQSTM1/p62 to distinguish aggregates, 2) DQ-BSA to label functional lysosomal proteolysis, and 3) LysoTracker for lysosomal acidity. Using high-content imaging and custom image analysis algorithms (CellProfiler pipelines for colocalization and puncta kinetics), we will quantify the proportion of GFP-LC3 puncta that colocalize with aggregates versus those that progress through an acidic, degradative pathway. Key perturbations will include mTORC inhibition (rapamycin), proteasome inhibition (MG132), and cocaine exposure (known from Levine's work to affect BECN1 and autophagy).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a rigorous, multi-parametric framework to validate GFP-LC3 puncta as true autophagosomes. It will provide the field with essential criteria and controls to prevent misinterpretation of autophagy flux. The findings will directly impact studies linking autophagy to substance abuse, neurodegeneration, and cancer where protein aggregation is co-morbid.
proposals.novelty: While concerns about LC3 specificity exist, no systematic, quantitative study has dissected the contribution of aggregates to the LC3 puncta assay under diverse pharmacological and stress conditions relevant to Dr. Levine's research themes (e.g., mTORC, cocaine). Integrating BECN1-interacting protein biology (GRB2) with this imaging validation is a novel angle.

proposals.title: Systematic Deconvolution of Off-Target Effects for Widely Used Autophagy Inhibitors 3-MA and Bafilomycin A1
proposals.motivation: The pharmacological inhibitors 3-methyladenine (3-MA) and bafilomycin A1 are foundational tools in autophagy research, used to block autophagosome formation and degradation, respectively. However, pre-2026 evidence suggests these compounds have significant off-target effects; 3-MA inhibits class I PI3K signaling and bafilomycin A1 disrupts lysosomal pH and other vesicular trafficking pathways. The continued reliance on these non-specific inhibitors risks misinterpretation of autophagy's role in cellular physiology and disease, including in contexts like addiction neuroscience where mTORC1 and protein homeostasis are implicated.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the cellular phenotypes commonly attributed to autophagy inhibition by 3-MA and bafilomycin A1 are, in significant part, mediated by their off-target effects on specific signaling nodes and vesicular trafficking pathways independent of the core autophagy machinery.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-pronged, mechanism-based approach in relevant cell lines (including neuronal models). First, we will use CRISPR/Cas9 to generate stable knockouts of core autophagy genes (e.g., ATG5, ATG7, BECN1) as genetic controls. We will treat these cells with 3-MA or bafilomycin A1 and perform comparative phospho-proteomic and interactomic analyses (using affinity purification-mass spectrometry) to map inhibitor-specific signaling perturbations distinct from genetic autophagy blockade. Key off-target pathways will be validated by monitoring activity of related proteins like GRB2 (a BECN1 interactor) and mTORC1. Finally, we will assay established autophagy markers (LC3-II flux, p62 degradation) alongside orthogonal readouts of the identified off-target pathways (e.g., endocytosis, primary ciliogenesis) to deconvolve their contributions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, high-resolution map of the specific and off-target effects of 3-MA and bafilomycin A1, establishing essential guidelines for their use and interpretation in autophagy experiments. It will clarify the true autophagy-dependent components of cellular processes studied with these tools and may identify novel regulatory nodes where autophagy intersects with other vesicular trafficking and signaling systems.
proposals.novelty: While the non-specificity of these drugs is acknowledged, a systematic, head-to-head comparison against precise genetic autophagy ablation to definitively partition their effects has not been comprehensively performed, particularly leveraging modern proteomic and genetic tools within the conceptual framework of Beth Levine's work on BECN1 interactors and regulatory mechanisms.

proposals.title: Super-resolution live-cell imaging reveals the nanoscale spatiotemporal dynamics of autophagosome formation and mTORC1 regulation
proposals.motivation: Autophagosome biogenesis is a fundamental but poorly resolved cellular process, with key steps like phagophore nucleation and expansion occurring below the diffraction limit. Pre-2026, the field relies heavily on static electron microscopy or diffraction-limited live imaging, missing critical nanoscale dynamics. Applying advanced super-resolution techniques from neuroscience could transform our mechanistic understanding of autophagy regulation by mTORC1 and BECN1-interacting proteins like GRB2.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that live-cell super-resolution microscopy will reveal distinct, ordered nanoscale assembly and disassembly dynamics of core autophagy machinery (e.g., ATG proteins, BECN1 complexes) at the phagophore, and that mTORC1 inhibition triggers specific, rapid changes in this spatial choreography.
proposals.method: We will employ lattice light-sheet microscopy (LLSM) and stimulated emission depletion (STED) microscopy for live-cell imaging. Cells (WT and autophagy-deficient) will express endogenously tagged autophagy proteins (e.g., LC3, ATG13, WIPI2) using CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing. For LLSM, we will use a lattice pattern generated by an optical spatial light modulator and image reconstruction via iterative deconvolution algorithms. For STED, a 592 nm depletion laser will be used with time-gated detection to enhance resolution. Dynamics will be quantified via single-particle tracking and correlation analysis algorithms.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first nanoscale, real-time visualization of autophagosome formation, defining the precise order of protein recruitment and membrane remodeling. It will directly test models of how mTORC1 signaling and BECN1-interacting proteins like GRB2 mechanistically control the initiation phase, moving the field from static snapshots to a dynamic movie of this critical degradative pathway.
proposals.novelty: While super-resolution is established in neuroscience for synaptic vesicle dynamics, its application to resolve the fast, sub-diffraction events of autophagosome biogenesis in living cells is novel. This approach uniquely bridges the structural biology of autophagy factors with their functional, real-time activity in a native cellular context.

proposals.title: Real-Time Nanoscale Tracking of Autophagosome Membrane Nucleation via Correlative iPALM and cryo-ET Imaging
proposals.motivation: Autophagosome biogenesis begins with the nucleation of a small isolation membrane (phagophore), yet the precise spatial and temporal dynamics of this event remain unresolved because conventional fluorescence microscopy lacks the resolution to visualize individual membrane nucleation sites. Understanding how BECN1-containing complexes, including the recently characterized GRB2-BECN1 interaction, orchestrate the initial membrane curvature at the nanometer scale is critical for deciphering autophagy regulation in health and disease. Resolving these earliest events would transform our mechanistic understanding of selective and bulk autophagy initiation.
proposals.hypothesis: Individual autophagosome membrane nucleation events occur at spatially defined ER-associated platforms where BECN1-VPS34 complex density exceeds a critical nanoscale threshold, and real-time super-resolution imaging can capture the transition from PI3P-enriched membrane puncta to curved phagophore intermediates with sub-20 nm precision.
proposals.method: We will combine interferometric photoactivated localization microscopy (iPALM) with correlative cryo-electron tomography (cryo-ET) to track BECN1 and ATG14L tagged with photoconvertible fluorescent proteins (mEos3.2) at ER-mitochondria contact sites in starved U2OS cells. Live iPALM will provide three-dimensional localization of single BECN1 molecules at 10-15 nm axial resolution, while time-lapse acquisition at 2-second intervals will capture nucleation dynamics. Cells will be vitrified at defined post-starvation timepoints and subjected to cryo-ET to resolve the ultrastructure of nascent membrane intermediates at the exact locations identified by iPALM. Quantitative spatial clustering analysis using Ripley's K-function and Voronoi tessellation will determine the critical local density of BECN1 complexes required to initiate membrane curvature, and perturbation of the GRB2-BECN1 interaction via point mutations will test whether GRB2 modulates nucleation site selection.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct nanoscale visualization of individual autophagosome membrane nucleation events in mammalian cells, establishing quantitative parameters—molecular density thresholds, curvature kinetics, and spatial relationship to ER subdomains—that currently exist only as theoretical predictions. The resulting framework will enable precise pharmacological targeting of autophagy initiation and clarify how upstream regulators like GRB2 tune the spatial probability of phagophore formation.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have used electron microscopy to observe mature autophagosomes or diffraction-limited fluorescence to track LC3 puncta, no existing approach captures the transition from molecular complex assembly to initial membrane deformation in real time at nanometer resolution. The correlative iPALM–cryo-ET pipeline applied specifically to BECN1-mediated nucleation, combined with single-molecule clustering analysis, is a non-obvious integration that bridges the gap between structural biology and live-cell autophagy dynamics.

ideas.title: Identify signals regulating Bcl-2-Beclin 1 dissociation
ideas.description: Characterize upstream signals or post-translational modifications that dynamically regulate the Bcl-2-Beclin 1 complex under stress.
ideas.sourceTitle: Bcl-2 antiapoptotic proteins inhibit Beclin 1-dependent autophagy.

ideas.title: MCF7 cells as atypical cancer model
ideas.description: Question whether MCF7 cells, which lack caspase-3 and have unusual apoptosis, represent typical breast cancer autophagy biology.
ideas.sourceTitle: Induction of autophagy and inhibition of tumorigenesis by beclin 1

ideas.title: Adapting neuroscience optogenetic spatial-temporal control strategies for tissue-specific autophagy gene deletion
ideas.description: In neuroscience, understanding the function of specific cell types within heterogeneous brain circuits required moving beyond simple knockouts to spatiotemporally precise genetic tools. The field solved this by developing intersectional strategies combining Cre and Flp recombinases under independent promoters, often pairing one constitutive tissue-specific driver with one inducible temporal driver (e.g., CreERT2 activated by tamoxifen). This dual-recombinase approach allowed silencing or activating genes only in cells where both promoters are active, achieving combinatorial specificity. Adapting this to autophagy research, we propose generating a dual-recombinase conditional beclin 1 allele (e.g., frt-flanked STOP cassette upstream of a floxed critical exon) that requires both Flp and Cre activity for gene deletion. By crossing these mice with tissue-specific Flp drivers (e.g., albumin-Flp for hepatocytes, nestin-Flp for neural progenitors) and tamoxifen-inducible CreERT2 lines, beclin 1 can be deleted exclusively in the target tissue and only upon tamoxifen administration at the chosen developmental stage. This intersectional approach overcomes limitations of single-recombinase systems—where promoter leakiness or broad expression windows confound results—and enables systematic dissection of autophagy's roles across tissues and developmental timepoints with the same rigor that neuroscience applies to circuit analysis.

ideas.title: Adapting drought-stress transcriptomics approaches to profile autophagy-deficient tumor gene expression
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from plant stress biology, where microarray profiling of autophagy-deficient (ATG-knockout) Arabidopsis under stress revealed compensatory transcriptional networks through weighted gene co-expression network analysis (WGCNA). Plant researchers discovered that when autophagy is genetically disrupted, plants activate alternative degradation pathways, ER stress responses, and metabolic reprogramming—identified not through simple differential expression but through network-based module detection that revealed coordinated gene programs and their hub regulators. Adapting this approach, we propose applying WGCNA and co-expression network construction to microarray profiles of beclin 1 haploinsufficient tumors alongside autophagy-competent controls. This would move beyond standard differential expression analysis to identify coherent transcriptional modules that tumors activate to compensate for partial autophagy loss, pinpoint hub transcription factors orchestrating these compensatory programs, and reveal whether tumor adaptations to autophagy deficiency parallel the conserved stress-response rewiring seen across kingdoms. The cross-kingdom analogy suggests that autophagy compensation may involve evolutionarily conserved network motifs, and the network approach is better suited than gene-list methods to capture the systems-level reorganization expected in haploinsufficient contexts where effects are subtle and distributed.

ideas.title: Tissue-specific haploinsufficiency mapping via enhancer-driven dosage sensitivity frameworks from developmental genetics
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from developmental genetics, where the tissue-specific effects of Shh haploinsufficiency were explained by mapping enhancer landscapes and morphogen concentration thresholds across tissues. In that field, researchers found that certain tissues (e.g., forebrain) operate near critical signaling thresholds with minimal buffering, making them uniquely sensitive to 50% gene dosage reduction, while other tissues possess regulatory elements that compensate for reduced dosage. Adapting this framework to beclin 1 and autophagy, the proposed research would: (1) perform ATAC-seq and ChIP-seq across breast, ovarian, prostate, and non-susceptible tissues to map the BECN1 cis-regulatory landscape and determine tissue-specific transcriptional output at heterozygous dosage; (2) quantify basal autophagy flux thresholds required for tumor suppression in each tissue using inducible knockdown systems titrated to varying beclin 1 levels; (3) profile the expression of compensatory autophagy pathway genes across tissues to identify which organs lack redundant buffering mechanisms; and (4) build a quantitative dosage-sensitivity model predicting tissue-specific tumor susceptibility based on enhancer architecture, basal autophagy demand, and compensatory gene network robustness. This approach reframes the question from 'why does beclin 1 loss cause cancer' to 'why do certain tissues operate near a critical autophagy threshold with insufficient regulatory buffering,' directly paralleling how the Shh field resolved analogous tissue-specificity puzzles.

ideas.title: Adapting single-cell transcriptomic deconvolution from immunology to map autophagy heterogeneity in beclin 1 haploinsufficient tumors
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from single-cell immunology, where the field transitioned from bulk immune profiling (e.g., flow cytometry panels, bulk RNA-seq of sorted populations) to single-cell multi-omic approaches (scRNA-seq, CITE-seq, spatial transcriptomics) to resolve the enormous functional heterogeneity among immune cells within tumors. That transition revealed previously invisible rare states, transitional phenotypes, and spatially organized niches that govern immune response. We propose adapting this exact analytical framework to the problem of autophagy heterogeneity in beclin 1 (BECN1) haploinsufficient tumors. Specifically, we would perform scRNA-seq on BECN1+/- tumor models and score each cell using curated autophagy gene modules (ATG genes, lysosomal markers, TFEB targets) to place cells on an autophagy activity spectrum. RNA velocity analysis would reveal whether cells are transitioning toward higher or lower autophagy states, identifying dynamic trajectories rather than static snapshots. Spatial transcriptomics (e.g., MERFISH or Visium) would then map these autophagy states onto tumor architecture to test whether autophagy-high cells cluster near hypoxic or nutrient-poor regions, while autophagy-low cells reside in well-vascularized zones. This approach would reveal whether BECN1 haploinsufficiency creates a uniform autophagy deficit or a mosaic of autophagy competencies that may drive intratumoral evolution, therapy resistance, and metabolic cooperation between subclones.

ideas.title: Alanine scanning and computational hotspot mapping of the Bcl-2/Beclin 1 interface inspired by integrin-extracellular matrix interaction mapping
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from structural studies of integrin-extracellular matrix protein interactions, where the challenge of selectively disrupting one protein-protein interaction without perturbing related interfaces was successfully addressed. In integrin biology, researchers used exhaustive alanine scanning mutagenesis combined with computational free energy perturbation (FEP) to identify binding hotspot residues at the integrin αVβ3–RGD interface. They discovered that a small number of residues contributed the majority of binding energy, and these hotspots were leveraged to design selective cyclic peptide inhibitors like cilengitide. Adapting this approach, we propose performing comprehensive alanine scanning across the Bcl-2 hydrophobic groove residues that engage Beclin 1's BH3-like domain, paired with FEP calculations and isothermal titration calorimetry validation. By comparing the energetic landscape of Bcl-2–Beclin 1 binding with that of Bcl-2–Bax and Bcl-2–Bak, we can identify residues that are energetically critical for Beclin 1 binding but dispensable for pro-apoptotic effector sequestration. These differential hotspots would then serve as pharmacophore anchors for designing stapled peptides or small molecules that selectively liberate Beclin 1 to activate autophagy without triggering apoptosis—a key therapeutic goal in cancer and neurodegeneration.

ideas.title: Functional driver classification of autophagy gene alterations across human cancers via integrated genomic-dependency analysis
ideas.description: This study systematically catalogues all autophagy-related gene alterations across TCGA pan-cancer cohorts but critically layers on functional dependency data from DepMap CRISPR/RNAi screens and transcriptomic autophagy flux signatures to classify each alteration as a likely driver or passenger event. A machine learning framework integrates copy number status, mutation frequency, co-occurrence with chromosomal instability markers, essentiality scores, and patient survival associations to rank autophagy genes by their probability of functional relevance in each cancer context. The top-ranked candidate driver alterations are then validated using isogenic knockout panels in representative cell lines, measuring autophagy flux, proliferation, and drug sensitivity to confirm causal roles. This approach overcomes the central limitation of purely descriptive pan-cancer surveys by ensuring that reported associations reflect genuine biological dependencies rather than genomic noise from widespread chromosomal instability.

ideas.title: Borrowing from developmental genetics: forward genetic screens in simple organisms to dissect autophagy-tumor suppression networks
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from the landmark forward genetic screens in developmental biology, where Drosophila and C. elegans were used to systematically dissect conserved signaling pathways through saturation mutagenesis. Just as Nüsslein-Volhard and Wieschaus identified the core components of Hedgehog, Wnt, and Notch signaling by screening for patterning defects in fly embryos, we propose conducting analogous unbiased genetic screens in non-mammalian tumor models to comprehensively map the autophagy-tumor suppression network. Specifically, EMS-based or transposon-mediated forward screens in Drosophila epithelial tumor models (e.g., RasV12/scribble clones in eye or wing discs) would identify modifiers that enhance or suppress autophagy-dependent tumor suppression. In parallel, CRISPR-based genetic screens in zebrafish BRAF-driven melanoma or tp53-mutant models would provide vertebrate validation. The short generation times, genetic tractability, and live-imaging capabilities of these organisms enable screening at a scale and speed impossible in mice. Conserved hits—genes whose loss shifts the autophagy-tumor interaction—would then be prioritized for mechanistic study in mammalian cell lines and mouse models. This approach leverages the proven power of model organism genetics to transform an incompletely understood process into a systematically mapped pathway, analogous to how developmental screens converted embryonic patterning from phenomenology into molecular mechanism.

ideas.description: Apply single-cell transcriptomic and proteomic profiling to classify cell death modalities at individual cell resolution.
ideas.sourceTitle: Molecular mechanisms of cell death: recommendations of the Nomenclature Committee on Cell Death 2018

ideas.title: Adapting calcium indicator imaging strategies to quantify autophagy flux in vivo
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from neuroscience, where genetically encoded calcium indicators (GCaMP) revolutionized in vivo measurement of neural activity. Neuroscientists overcame the problem of quantifying rapid, transient intracellular signals in deep tissue by combining genetically encoded fluorescent sensors with cell-type-specific expression, surgical optical access (cranial windows), two-photon microscopy, and ratiometric calibration strategies. We adapt this solution to autophagy research by deploying tandem-fluorescent LC3 reporters (mCherry-GFP-LC3) via AAV vectors with tumor-conditional promoters into beclin 1 mutant tumor-bearing mice. Intravital two-photon imaging through surgically implanted dorsal skinfold or abdominal imaging windows enables repeated, non-destructive visualization of autophagosome formation and maturation. The dual-fluorophore ratiometric readout—where GFP fluorescence is quenched upon autolysosome acidification while mCherry remains stable—provides a built-in calibration analogous to ratiometric GCaMP variants, allowing conversion of imaging data into quantitative autophagy flux metrics. This approach enables longitudinal tracking of how beclin 1 loss alters autophagy dynamics during tumor progression and in response to therapy, overcoming the snapshot limitations of traditional immunohistochemistry.

ideas.title: Hitchhiker deletion mapping: Borrowing linkage disequilibrium methods from population genetics to distinguish passenger from driver gene losses
ideas.description: This idea draws a direct analogy between the passenger-versus-driver gene deletion problem in cancer genomics and the causal-variant-versus-LD-proxy problem in population genetics. In GWAS, a statistically significant SNP may simply be co-inherited with a nearby causal variant rather than being functional itself; the field resolved this ambiguity using fine-mapping frameworks that assign posterior inclusion probabilities to each variant in a correlated region by modeling joint effects, leveraging LD structure, and incorporating functional annotations as Bayesian priors. We propose adapting this approach to somatic copy-number alterations on chromosome 17q21. Using pan-cancer datasets (TCGA, ICGC, PCAWG), each gene in the minimally deleted region—BECLIN1, BRCA1, and flanking loci—would be treated as a candidate 'causal deletion' analogous to a candidate causal SNP. A Bayesian fine-mapping model would estimate the posterior probability that loss of each individual gene contributes to tumor fitness, conditional on the losses of all other genes in the segment. Functional priors would include gene essentiality scores from DepMap CRISPR screens, expression-fitness correlations, known roles in autophagy or DNA repair, and somatic mutation frequency. If BECLIN1's posterior inclusion probability is negligible once conditioned on BRCA1 loss—mirroring how a non-causal SNP drops out of a credible set once the true causal variant is accounted for—this would provide rigorous statistical evidence that BECLIN1 monoallelic deletion is a passenger event hitchhiking on BRCA1-driven selection. This cross-disciplinary transfer leverages two decades of sophisticated statistical genetics methodology to resolve a longstanding debate in cancer biology about the role of autophagy gene loss in tumorigenesis.
